Now we're going to talk about how to write a sales page that rakes in as much as $10 million without being salesy or pushy or sleazy. So here's what we're about to discover. You're going to learn the seven persuasion principles that make your products sell. You're going to learn how to beat the blank page blues. You know that feeling where you're looking at the screen or at the paper thinking, I have no idea what to say. We're going to solve that problem for you. I'll give you the sales copy framework behind the actual sales page that has generated over $10 million in sales and how to crank out a money-making sales page in a single afternoon. Sounds crazy, but it is possible. And we'll start with the seven persuasion principles that make your products sell. Persuasion principle number one, it's all about them, not about you. You've heard this before, but it's really important to understand that you, your product, your service, your company are not the hero of the story. They are the hero of the story. As Don likes to explain it, you are not Luke Skywalker, you are Yoda, and Luke Skywalker is the hero. That's your customer. And you need to make sure they stay in the hero position. Persuasion principle number two, to make it about them, you have to talk about you. This seems like a contradiction, but really it isn't. You just have to make sure that when you tell a story that may be about you, it may be your personal story, you tell it in a way that is meaningful to your customer. Think of it this way, when I was in high school, I had my first car. It was a lime green 1977 Chevrolet Chevette. It was dead sexy. And it had a hole in the passenger side floorboard so that when it was raining, and I'd be driving down the highway, rain would spray up into the cabin, which was an appealing prospect if I wanted to go on a date with someone. And also made the car smell great. So this was not a date getting machine. Now pause for just a moment and think about this. When I started telling you the story about my first car, what did you think about? You probably thought about your first car, maybe you didn't have a terrible car like I did, maybe you had a Maserati, I don't know. But whatever your first car was, you were thinking about that. You have to remember that when you tell stories, people are automatically either inserting themselves into the story, or they're thinking about their story that matches. So just keep that in mind and make sure that you're making a point by telling your story. The great Jim Rohn often said, never tell a story without having a point, and never make a point without telling a story. Persuasion principle number three. Great copy does four things. It addresses a problem, makes a promise, offers proof, and ends with a proposal. So you have to start with the problem that you're helping people solve. And again, that's about them. It's not about you. It's not about how great your company is, how great your product is. It's really about the problem that they are facing that causes them pain. You're making a promise. The promise is how you solve the problem, and you offer proof that your promise actually works. And then you end with a proposal. You're asking for the sale. Couldn't be simpler. Persuasion principle number four. Your copy must be useful and valuable in and of itself. This is one of my pet peeves about most marketing material that you see these days. It's usually so self-focused on the company, on the product, and it's usually all sales pitch and no value. I feel that we have an obligation to our customers and our prospects. If they're going to give us the investment of their time, their most valuable asset, we need to make sure that they get something in return, even if they never buy from us. We need to make sure our marketing material is valuable in and of itself. I think marketing should be so valuable that if people don't buy from us, they still walk away from reading our sales copy or watching our sales video or reading our sales email, and they should be able to walk away and think, well, that was really worth my time. Marketing is something we do for people, not something we do to people. Persuasion principle number five. There is no copy that is too long. There is only copy that is too boring. Now, sometimes sales copy tends to be lengthy, and people ask me why, and I explain, well, it's because we're not there communicating face to face. If I were giving a sales presentation to you in person, I would be able to read your body language. You'd ask questions. I'd be able to see the expression on your face. And so I would know how to communicate in a way that was tailored just to you, just to what your pain points or your frustrations were or the points where you became confused. But on the internet, we're not in person, so we don't have that luxury. So we have to answer all their questions. We have to deal with all their objections. We have to give them all the information they need in order to make a buying decision ahead of time. That's why copy tends to be long. And it isn't long if it's interesting. I want you to think about something. Would you read a work that was, say, 4,101 pages long? Well, that's how long all the Harry Potter books, the original series, that's how long they are in this particular edition, 4,101 pages. And I guarantee you not a single Harry Potter fan said at the end of the last book, that was too long. I wish it would have been over a long time ago. Many of them started over at page one again because they didn't want it to be over because it was interesting. So no copy is too long, only too boring. Persuasion principle number six. You must present the reader with a preponderance of proof. It's not enough just to say studies show or our tests indicate or even to just say our product or our service is great or even to say our customers think it's great. You have to show them proof. You need words from your customers. We're going to be talking in another module about how to get testimonials. That's your proof. And you want not just a little bit of proof. You want a preponderance of proof so that there's no question in your prospects mind of whether this works or not. They know it works. It has to work because you've proven it works. And finally, persuasion principle number seven. Great copy leads the reader to a fork in the road. What I mean is they have to make a decision. Copy works if the prospect makes a decision at the end of reading the copy. It doesn't matter really if the answer is no, I'm not going to buy or yes, I am going to buy. If you can lead them to making a decision and your product has value to bring, you'll get enough yeses so that your business will be profitable. You know, the great philosopher, Yogi Berra, said, when you come to a fork in the road, take it. And that's what you want your prospects to do. When they read your copy, when they watch your sales video, you want to make sure that they have to make a decision. Either I'm going to engage with this company and buy their product, their service, or I'm not. But you don't want them to walk away saying, I'll think about it. Because what I'll think about it means is they'll be off to something else and they'll never come back. So you've got to make them make a decision. Okay, how do we beat the blank page blues? How do we beat the terror of sitting in front of that blank screen or that blank paper, not knowing exactly what to write or what to say? Well, it's very easy. It's very simple. Don't start with a blank page. Now, you might say, well, what do I start with, Ray? You want to jumpstart your copy by using the tools that I'm about to show you. You need to start collecting what we call in the copywriting business swipe files. So don't be deceived by this name. Swipe file does not mean that you're stealing other people's copy. But you should collect copy that is very effective, that makes you want to buy, especially copy that makes you buy. Because that copy works and you use it for inspiration. Every great copywriter I know has a swipe file, a file of ads that worked either worked for them and caused them to buy or worked en masse for a large audience of people and they know those ads were successful. So one thing to be careful of is you want to make sure that the ads you add and the copy you add to your swipe file actually worked. It can't be just something that you like, that you think looks good. You have to make sure that it actually sold product. The next tool in our arsenal for jumpstarting your copy is to stack blocks of copy. As you write more and more copy, you will become the possessor of a number of chunks of copy that you use over and over again. Every time you write a piece of copy, you'll have certain language that you use every time. And you start collecting those blocks so that you end up in a place where you can really assemble large pieces of every copy project ahead of time just because you go back to your library of copy that you wrote. You're not borrowing somebody else's copy, but you write a certain kind of guarantee and you know you can reuse that over and over again. You just pull from your library of copy and you can assemble copy and not have to start from scratch. When all else fails, write the offer. You know what you're selling, presumably. You know what the components are. You know how much it costs. So write the offer and that will jumpstart the rest of your copy. Finally, you want to use an outline. You know, great copy has a structure. It has a format and all great copy really follows basically the same outline, just like all great stories follow the same outline. Have that structure available ahead of time and you won't be starting from a blank page. And the good news is we're going to give you the copy framework that you can use as your outline.
